meta.description: Tourism knowledge base for Nepal Rural Tourism chatbot

greeting:
Namaste! 🙏 I'm your Nepal Rural Tourism assistant. I can help you with:
• Best time to visit destinations
• Transport and trekking routes
• Homestays and accommodation
• Local food and culture
• Safety tips and budget info

What would you like to know?
Namaste! Welcome to the Gandaki Rural Tourism guide. Ask me anything about destinations, trekking, transport, food, or culture. How can I help you today?
responses.best_time_to_visit_generic:
The best times to visit rural Nepal are:

🌸 Spring (March–May): Clear skies, rhododendrons in bloom, ideal for trekking.
🍂 Autumn (September–November): Best visibility, stable weather, peak trekking season.

Monsoon (June–August) brings heavy rain but lush green landscapes. Winter (December–February) is cold at altitude but peaceful and crowd-free.
responses.transport_generic:
Getting around the Gandaki region:

🚗 Jeep/taxi from Pokhara: Most villages are 45 min–3 hours by road.
🚌 Local bus: Cheaper but slower; available to most trailheads.
🥾 Trekking: Many destinations are best reached on foot along well-marked trails.

Tip: Hire a local guide for first-time trekkers — they know the trails and can assist in emergencies.
responses.homestay_generic:
Rural homestays are the heart of community tourism in Nepal. Most villages offer:

🏠 Traditional Gurung or Magar stone houses
🛏️ Clean rooms with local meals included (typically NRP 1,000–2,500 per night with dinner and breakfast)
👨‍👩‍👧 Family-hosted — a great way to experience local culture

Book in advance during peak season (Oct–Nov, Mar–Apr) as rooms fill up fast.
responses.food_generic:
Local cuisine in the Gandaki region:

🍱 Dal Bhat: The staple meal — lentils, rice, vegetables and curries. Usually included in homestay packages.
🫖 Butter tea or milk tea with local snacks
🥘 Dhido: Buckwheat or millet porridge — traditional and nutritious
🍺 Tongba: Fermented millet beer, a local specialty in higher villages

Vegetarian options are almost always available. Be careful with drinking water — use purification tablets or buy sealed bottles.
responses.culture_etiquette_generic:
Cultural tips for rural Nepal:

🙏 Greet with 'Namaste' and a slight bow
👟 Remove shoes before entering temples and homes
👗 Dress modestly, especially near religious sites
📸 Always ask before photographing people
🚶 Walk clockwise around stupas and mani walls
🤝 Accept offerings or food with your right hand
⛩️ Do not touch religious objects unless invited

Locals are warm and welcoming — a small gesture of respect goes a long way.
responses.safety_generic:
Safety tips for rural tourism in Nepal:

✅ Nepal's rural areas are generally very safe for tourists
🏔️ Altitude sickness: Ascend gradually, stay hydrated, descend immediately if you feel very unwell
🧭 Always carry a paper map or offline map — mobile signal is patchy
💊 Bring a basic first aid kit and any prescription medicines
🔒 Keep a photocopy of your passport and travel insurance
📞 Emergency numbers: Police 100, Ambulance 102, Tourist Police 01-4247041

Trekking with a local guide significantly reduces risk.
responses.budget_generic:
Rough daily budget guide for rural Gandaki:

💚 Budget (NRP 2,000–4,000 / day): Homestay with meals, local transport
💛 Medium (NRP 4,000–8,000 / day): Comfortable guesthouse, guide hire
🔴 Premium (NRP 8,000+ / day): Guided multi-day trek with porter and teahouse stays

Trekking permits: ACAP permit NRP 3,000 (foreigners), TIMS card NRP 2,000.
Most village shops accept Nepali rupees only — bring enough cash from Pokhara.
responses.trekking_generic:
Trekking in the Gandaki region:

🥾 Popular routes: Ghandruk Loop, Panchase Trek, Mardi Himal, Annapurna Base Camp
📋 Permits needed: ACAP (Annapurna Conservation Area Permit) + TIMS card
🗺️ Difficulty: From easy day hikes (Dhampus, Sarangkot) to challenging multi-day treks (ABC)
🧭 Guides & porters: Recommended for first-timers; hire through Pokhara agencies
👟 What to bring: Layered clothing, rain gear, trekking poles, water purification

Best trekking season: October–November and March–May.
responses.recommendation_help:
Great question! The best way to get personalised recommendations is to use the Recommend tab (the slider icon in the bottom navigation). You can set your:
• Preferred activity (trekking, culture, relaxation…)
• Budget (budget / medium / premium)
• Season you plan to visit
• Trip vibe (peaceful, adventure, cultural…)

The AI will suggest the best matching destinations from our database. Want me to suggest a few popular ones right now?
responses.emergency_help:
🚨 Emergency contacts in Nepal:

📞 Police: 100
🚑 Ambulance: 102
🏔️ Mountain Rescue: CAAN Helicopter Rescue — contact through your trekking agency
🏥 Tourist Police: 01-4247041
🌐 Pokhara Hospital: +977-61-520066

If you are on a trek and need help: stay calm, signal other trekkers, and use your emergency whistle (3 blasts = distress signal). Your lodge owner can also help contact rescue services.

⚠️ Always carry travel insurance that covers helicopter evacuation.
responses.offline_help:
This app is designed to work offline! Here's how:

📦 All destination data is stored locally on your device — no internet needed to browse.
🗺️ The map uses locally cached tiles for the Pokhara/Gandaki region.
⭐ Saved destinations are stored on your device.
🤖 Recommendations use an offline AI engine that runs entirely on your phone.

Internet is only needed for: AI backend recommendations (optional), translation, and fetching real-time updates.
responses.smalltalk: You're welcome! 😊 Let me know if you have any other questions about Nepal's rural tourism.
responses.fallback:
I'm not sure I understood that. I can help with:
• Best time to visit 🗓️
• Transport and routes 🚗
• Homestays and accommodation 🏠
• Food and cuisine 🍱
• Culture and etiquette 🙏
• Safety tips 🛡️
• Budget info 💰
• Trekking advice 🥾
• Nearby places 📍
• Destination details 🗺️

Try asking something like: 'When is the best time to visit Ghandruk?' or 'How do I get to Sikles?'
